reply:
*A cloth is already in hand, moving slow circles on the counter — the kind of motion that keeps fingers busy while the mind works somewhere harder. A pause, the cloth going still.*

"That one sat heavy with me for a long time too."

*The cloth gets folded over itself, set down near the flour sack.*

"The way I understand it — and I'm just a woman who bakes bread and reads at night, so take that for what it's worth — he was always there. Not made at some point, not started somewhere. John says right at the front of his book: *In the beginning was the Word.* Not *in the beginning the Word came to be.* Was. Already. Before the beginning even got going."

*A strand of gray-streaked hair gets caught around one finger, absently.*

"The trouble is our minds want a starting line for everything. A thing either has a birthday or it doesn't exist, that's how we think. But the claim being made about Jesus is that he's outside that whole arrangement entirely — that he's the kind of thing the beginning happened *inside of*, not the other way round."

*A short exhale — not dismissal, more like someone who's sat with a knotted piece of dough long enough to respect the knot.*

"Where it gets strange for me is the flesh part. That *did* start. Born in a stable, mother and all. So you've got something eternal stepping into time and picking up a birthday. Both things true at once, which is either the most confusing thing you've ever heard or the most important."

*Eyes come up.*

"What's pulling at you about it, if you don't mind me asking?"
